did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper implemented and evaluated multiple Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures for the classification of skin cancer. It trained 10 CNN models on images from the ISIC dataset (categorized into 7 classes) using feature engineering and image augmentation to handle class imbalance, selecting the best architecture based on metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors implemented and evaluated 10 different Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures on the ISIC dataset for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodologies including feature engineering, image augmentation, and training on different input sizes (64x64 and 128x128), and reports original results such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores in Tables IV and V. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary contribution is the new experimental validation of model architectures, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a conference paper).

title: Diagnosis of Skin Cancer Using Feature Engineering Techniques
journal: 2021 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computing, Communication Control and Networking (ICACCCN)
brief_summary: This study addresses the early diagnosis of skin cancer by implementing and evaluating ten different Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures. The models were trained on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, which was categorized into seven classes and balanced using feature engineering and image augmentation to handle class imbalance. Five architectures were trained on 64x64 input images and five on 128x128 input images, with performance assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The results indicated that Architecture 4, utilizing 128x128 input size, achieved the highest accuracy of 0.9834 and superior performance across all evaluation metrics compared to the other models. The authors conclude that this specific architecture is the most optimal for diagnosing skin cancer with high stability and low computational cost.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (7 classes: Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements and evaluates 10 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures for categorical classification of skin cancer. The dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) was categorized into 7 classes. The models were trained to predict discrete diagnostic labels, and performance was assessed using classification metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. Architecture 4 achieved the highest accuracy of 0.9834.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (2018 Challenge)
datasets_used.description: Data retrieved from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) challenge, specifically the dataset published during 2018 and 2019. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions categorized into 7 classes. The raw dataset was imbalanced and underwent feature engineering and image augmentation (using Keras Image Data Generator) to balance the classes for training.

use.training: Used to train 10 CNN architectures (5 on 64x64 input size, 5 on 128x128 input size) after balancing via feature engineering and image augmentation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the trained CNN architectures using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model selection and comparison were based on descriptive performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, Maximum Validation Accuracy (MVA), Least Validation Cross Entropy Loss (LVCEL), and training time.

experiment_64x64_input_size.notes: Architecture 2 was selected as the best for 64x64 input size based on Maximum Validation Accuracy (MVA) and Least Validation Cross Entropy Loss (LVCEL). Architecture 3 had the lowest accuracy among 64x64 models.

experiment_128x128_input_size.notes: Architecture 4 was selected as the best for 128x128 input size. It achieved the highest accuracy (0.9834) and lowest LVCEL (0.0610). Architecture 2 had the lowest accuracy among 128x128 models.

comparison_of_selected_architectures.notes: Architecture 4 of 128x128 input size performed better than Architecture 2 of 64x64 in terms of all metric evaluations (MVA, LVCEL, Precision, Recall, F1-Score). Architecture 4 is concluded as the most optimal structure for diagnosing skin cancer disease.

architecture_details.layers: Custom architectures composed of Convolutional Layers (CL), Artificial/Dense Layers (AL), Flatten layers, and Fully Connected layers. 5 distinct architectures were implemented for each input size (10 total).

training_details.augmentation: Image augmentation using Keras Image Data Generator to handle class imbalance

preprocessing_techniques: Feature engineering to handle class imbalance

Image augmentation using Keras Image Data Generator (including shear, zoom, rescale, flipping, whitening, random rotations, shifts, brightness increment, width shifting, and height shifting) to balance the dataset
Resizing input images to 64x64 and 128x128 dimensions

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Image Augmentation via Keras Image Data Generator
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to balance the imbalanced dataset by increasing minority classes. Techniques included shear, zoom, rescale, flipping, whitening, random rotations, shifts, brightness increment, width shifting, and height shifting.

conclusions: Architecture 4, utilizing a 128x128 input image size, achieved the highest accuracy of 0.9834 and superior performance across all evaluation metrics (Precision, Recall, F1-score) compared to other tested CNN architectures.
Among the architectures trained on 64x64 input images, Architecture 2 was identified as the best performer with an accuracy of 0.9768.
The study concludes that Architecture 4 is the most optimal structure for diagnosing skin cancer due to its high stability and low computational cost relative to its performance.
Feature engineering and image augmentation were critical techniques for handling the class imbalance in the ISIC dataset, enabling effective training of the CNN models.

There is a direct proportionality between architectural complexity (number of parameters) and computational training time, with 128x128 input sizes requiring significantly longer training times than 64x64 inputs.

Early diagnosis of skin cancer, particularly Melanoma, is beneficial as early detection leads to a 95% recovery rate, and machine learning/deep learning approaches offer a feasible and economical solution for this task.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant limitation regarding generalizability, stating that results 'may not be generalized for a very big amount of data, having a lot of variation' due to the specific dataset and experimental conditions.
The dataset was artificially balanced using image augmentation (Keras Image Data Generator) to handle class imbalance, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence distributions.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the testing split of the merged ISIC 2018/2019 dataset.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between architectures, relying instead on descriptive metrics.
Training times varied significantly with input size (e.g., Architecture 4 at 128x128 took 9468 seconds vs. 2490 seconds for Architecture 2 at 64x64), impacting computational efficiency.

The authors suggest that future work should utilize GANs for data generation to potentially improve generalization and stability, implying current augmentation methods may be insufficient for broader applicability.
